5.1.title: Arjuna's either-or framing of the two paths
5.1.body: Arjuna addresses Kṛṣṇa with two accusative direct objects — संन्यासं कर्मणाम् and पुनर्योगम् — both governed by शंससि (लट्, मध्यम-पुरुष, एकवचन of √शंस्, 'you praise'). The षष्ठी कर्मणाम् ('renunciation of actions') makes संन्यास a genitive-compound target, and यच्छ्रेय एतयोरेकम् ('whichever one of these two is better') uses एतयोः (षष्ठी द्विवचन) to pin the choice down to exactly two.

5.1.title: ब्रूहि सुनिश्चितम् — Arjuna wants a verdict, not a discourse
5.1.body: The imperative ब्रूहि (लोट्, √ब्रू) with the adverbial सुनिश्चितम् ('decisively, with certainty') is a demand for a single ruling — तत्...एकम्...मे ब्रूहि, 'tell me that one thing'. The verse frames Chapter 5's whole project: Arjuna treats संन्यास and कर्मयोग as mutually exclusive, and Kṛṣṇa will spend the chapter dismantling that exclusivity.

5.2.title: निःश्रेयसकरौ — both paths bear the same fruit
5.2.body: The compound निःश्रेयसकरौ is a बहुव्रीहि-style तत्पुरुष ('producers of the highest good'), and its dual ending -औ agrees with the dual subject संन्यासः कर्मयोगश्च. Both are predicated as उभौ ('both together') doing the same work — Kṛṣṇa refuses Arjuna's either-or before correcting the ranking.

5.2.title: विशिष्यते — कर्मयोग wins by पञ्चमी comparison
5.2.body: कर्मसंन्यासात् is पञ्चमी विभक्ति of comparison ('than the renunciation-of-action'), and विशिष्यते (लट् आत्मनेपद of वि+√शिष्, 'is distinguished/excels') is the comparative verb. The grammar itself encodes the verdict: of the two equally-fruit-bearing paths, कर्मयोग is the one that excels — Kṛṣṇa answers 5.1's question with a ranked equality.

5.3.title: नित्यसंन्यासी defined by what one does not do
5.3.body: The real संन्यासी is identified by two negated finite verbs in लट् — न द्वेष्टि न काङ्क्षति ('neither hates nor longs'). ज्ञेयः (gerundive of √ज्ञा, 'to be known as') and the predicate noun नित्यसंन्यासी redefine renunciation away from external robe-and-forest and onto the internal absence of द्वेष and काङ्क्षा.

5.3.title: निर्द्वन्द्वः — the ablative of release from bondage
5.3.body: निर्द्वन्द्वः is a बहुव्रीहि ('one for whom the pairs-of-opposites are gone'), and बन्धात् is पञ्चमी विभक्ति of separation governed by प्रमुच्यते (लट् आत्मनेपद of प्र+√मुच्, 'is fully released'). The hi (हि) marks the logical consequence: freedom from द्वन्द्व causally produces freedom from बन्ध — सुखम् adverbially qualifies the manner of release.

5.4.title: बालाः vs पण्डिताः — who splits the paths
5.4.body: The contrast is built on two nominative-plural subjects: बालाः ('the childish') प्रवदन्ति ('declare') साङ्ख्ययोगौ पृथक् ('Sāṅkhya and Yoga as separate'), but न पण्डिताः ('not the wise'). साङ्ख्ययोगौ is द्वितीया dual द्वन्द्व, and पृथक् is the adverb that the childish wrongly apply to it.

5.4.title: एकमप्यास्थितः — one path yields both fruits
5.4.body: एकमपि ('even one') is accusative qualifying the implied path, आस्थितः is past-passive-participle of आ+√स्था ('having taken one's stand on'), and उभयोः is षष्ठी द्विवचन ('of both'). The finite verb विन्दते (लट् आत्मनेपद of √विद्, 'obtains') governs फलम् — taking one's stand on one path correctly fetches the फल of both.

5.5.title: तत्...गम्यते — the same destination by either road
5.5.body: Two passive finite verbs run parallel: यत्...प्राप्यते (लट् कर्मणि of प्र+√आप्, 'is reached') with instrumental साङ्ख्यैः, and तत्...गम्यते (लट् कर्मणि of √गम्, 'is gone-to') with instrumental योगैः. The तृतीया विभक्ति plurals frame the two paths as means, and the correlative यत्...तत् welds their destination — स्थानम् — into one.

5.5.title: स पश्यति — the seer is the one who sees the unity
5.5.body: एकम् is the predicate accusative ('as one'), and the cleft यः पश्यति स पश्यति ('he who sees, he [truly] sees') makes 'seeing' itself the test. The repetition of पश्यति (लट्, √दृश्) is not redundancy — the first is empirical, the second is darśana: only the one who collapses साङ्ख्य and योग into एकम् has actually seen.

5.6.title: दुःखमाप्तुम् — renunciation without yoga is painful
5.6.body: आप्तुम् is the तुमुन्-infinitive of √आप् ('to attain'), and दुःखम् is its adverbial accusative ('with difficulty'). अयोगतः is पञ्चमी विभक्ति governed by the ablative -तस् suffix ('from absence-of-yoga') — bare संन्यास, divorced from कर्मयोग, is grammatically marked as painful-to-reach.

5.6.title: नचिरेण ब्रह्म अधिगच्छति — yoga's quick verdict
5.6.body: योगयुक्तः is the bahuvrīhi subject ('he who is joined-with-yoga'), मुनिः the apposition, and ब्रह्म the accusative object of अधिगच्छति (लट् of अधि+√गम्, 'attains'). The तृतीया adverb नचिरेण ('in no long time') is the punchline — the कर्मयोगी reaches the same ब्रह्म that the dry संन्यासी reaches दुःखम्, but quickly.

5.7.title: Four bahuvrīhi compounds stacking the yogī's profile
5.7.body: The verse lines up four nominative-singular बहुव्रीहि epithets — योगयुक्तः, विशुद्धात्मा, विजितात्मा, जितेन्द्रियः ('joined-in-yoga, of-purified-self, of-conquered-self, of-conquered-senses'). Each compound has an internal कर्मधारय or तत्पुरुष structure with the relational sense 'one-whose-X-is-Y' — a stacked portrait of the agent before the predicate even arrives.

5.7.title: कुर्वन्नपि न लिप्यते — the acting-yet-unstained paradox
5.7.body: कुर्वन् is the शतृ-participle of √कृ ('while doing'), and अपि intensifies the concession ('even while acting'). The finite लट् verb न लिप्यते (आत्मनेपद, √लिप्, 'is not smeared') announces Chapter 5's central paradox: सर्वभूतात्मभूतात्मा ('he whose self has become the self of all beings') acts but does not accumulate karmic residue.

5.8.title: नैव किञ्चित्करोमीति — the tattvavid's first-person verdict
5.8.body: इति brackets a direct first-person quotation: न एव किञ्चित् करोमि ('I do not do anything at all'). मन्येत is विधिलिङ् (आत्मनेपद, √मन्, 'he should think'), making this not a description but a prescriptive stance the तत्त्ववित् ('knower-of-truth') is to hold — the mood itself encodes a normative ought.

5.8.title: Seven शतृ-participles — perception itself is involuntary
5.8.body: The verse drops a chain of seven nominative-singular present participles: पश्यन्, शृण्वन्, स्पृशन्, जिघ्रन्, अश्नन्, गच्छन्, स्वपन्, श्वसन् ('seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, going, sleeping, breathing'). All are उपपद-less standalone शतृ forms qualifying the same subject — every basic sensory and physiological act is grammatically demoted from main verb to backgrounded participle while the mind holds न करोमि.

5.9.title: More participles — speech, evacuation, even blinking
5.9.body: The catalogue continues: प्रलपन्, विसृजन्, गृह्णन्, उन्मिषन्, निमिषन् ('talking, releasing, grasping, opening-the-eyes, closing-the-eyes'). The deliberate inclusion of involuntary उन्मिषन्निमिषन् pushes the point — even the autonomic eyeblink is not 'my doing'; it is sense-organ activity, not self-activity.

5.9.title: इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेषु वर्तन्ते — the locative resolution
5.9.body: The finite clause arrives at last: इन्द्रियाणि (nom. pl.) वर्तन्ते (लट् आत्मनेपद of √वृत्, 'turn/move about') इन्द्रियार्थेषु (सप्तमी विभक्ति, 'among sense-objects'). धारयन् (शतृ of √धृ, 'holding [this thought]') frames the whole as a meditative posture — the इति-quote of 5.8 is grounded in the locative metaphysics that senses move among objects on their own.

5.10.title: ब्रह्मणि आधाय — dedicating actions in the locative
5.10.body: ब्रह्मणि is सप्तमी विभक्ति ('in/upon Brahman'), and आधाय is the ल्यप्-absolutive of आ+√धा ('having placed'). The absolutive सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा ('having abandoned attachment') stacks a second prior action — both ल्यप्/क्त्वा forms must complete before the finite करोति (लट्, √कृ) can apply, so karmic non-binding is structurally pre-conditioned.

5.10.title: पद्मपत्रमिवाम्भसा — the lotus-leaf simile in तृतीया
5.10.body: इव marks the simile, and अम्भसा is तृतीया विभक्ति ('by water') governed by the implied 'is-not-wetted'. The compound पद्मपत्रम् is nominative subject of the comparison, and लिप्यते न (लट् कर्मणि of √लिप्) is the shared predicate — the यः-clause's actor and the lotus-leaf are syntactically parallel: both touched, neither stained.

5.11.title: Four instrumentals — the full inventory of action's instruments
5.11.body: कायेन, मनसा, बुद्ध्या, केवलैरिन्द्रियैः — four तृतीया विभक्ति nouns ('by body, by mind, by intellect, by even the bare senses'). The intensifier केवलैः ('even just') extends the list to its minimum unit, making clear that every conceivable instrument of action is covered by the yogī's working modality.

5.11.title: आत्मशुद्धये — the dative of the goal of action
5.11.body: आत्मशुद्धये is चतुर्थी विभक्ति ('for the purification of the self'), the तत्पुरुष goal-case governed by कुर्वन्ति (लट्, √कृ, 'they do'). सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा (absolutive) is again the structural precondition — the dative reframes karma not as productive of result but as instrumental to inner purification.

5.12.title: युक्तः vs अयुक्तः — the structural opposition of the verse
5.12.body: Two nominative-singular subjects bookend the verse: युक्तः ('the yoked one') ... आप्नोति शान्तिम् नैष्ठिकीम् (लट्, 'attains lasting peace'), and अयुक्तः ('the unyoked') ... निबध्यते (लट् कर्मणि of नि+√बन्ध्, 'is bound'). कर्मफलं त्यक्त्वा (absolutive) is what the first does; कामकारेण (तृतीया, 'by acting-on-desire') is the means by which the second is undone.

5.12.title: फले सक्तः — the locative of attachment
5.12.body: फले is सप्तमी विभक्ति ('to/upon the fruit'), and सक्तः is the क्त-participle of √सञ्ज् ('attached, stuck'). The locative encodes that attachment is locational — the unyoked agent is grammatically pinned to the fruit-of-action, while युक्तः has त्यक्त्वा-released it and so cannot be pinned anywhere.

5.13.title: नवद्वारे पुरे — the nine-gated city in सप्तमी
5.13.body: नवद्वारे पुरे is सप्तमी विभक्ति ('in the city-of-nine-gates'), with नवद्वार a बहुव्रीहि qualifying the locative पुर. देही (nom., 'the embodied one') is the dweller; आस्ते (लट् आत्मनेपद of √आस्, 'sits/abides') is the finite verb — the body is a city the self merely resides in, not operates.

5.13.title: नैव कुर्वन्न कारयन् — the doer-and-causer doubly negated
5.13.body: Two शतृ-participles are negated in series: कुर्वन् ('doing') and कारयन् (causative participle, 'causing-to-do'). सर्वकर्माणि मनसा संन्यस्य (ल्यप्-absolutive of सं+नि+√अस्, 'having renounced all actions by the mind') is the prior condition. The vaśī ('controlled one') is neither agent nor instigator — both rungs of the कर्तृ-ladder are syntactically cancelled.

5.14.title: प्रभुः न सृजति — the Lord is not the source
5.14.body: प्रभुः (nom., 'the Lord [i.e., the embodied self]') is the subject of three accusative objects: कर्तृत्वम् ('agency'), कर्माणि ('actions'), कर्मफलसंयोगम् ('union-with-fruit-of-action'). All three are governed by न ... सृजति (लट्, √सृज्, 'does not create') — the self is grammatically stripped of every link in the causal chain.

5.14.title: स्वभावस्तु प्रवर्तते — nature alone is the actor
5.14.body: The contrastive तु flips the subject: स्वभावः (nom., 'inherent nature') प्रवर्तते (लट् आत्मनेपद of प्र+√वृत्, 'proceeds/operates'). The finite verb shifts from the negated सृजति of the Lord to the affirmative प्रवर्तते of स्वभाव — the grammar itself relocates causal agency from puruṣa to prakṛti.

5.15.title: न आदत्ते — the Vibhu takes on no one's karma
5.15.body: आदत्ते is लट् आत्मनेपद of आ+√दा ('takes/accepts'), and कस्यचित् is षष्ठी विभक्ति ('of anyone'). Both पापम् and सुकृतम् are accusative direct objects — Vibhuḥ (nom., 'the all-pervading one') is grammatically refused both transfers, the genitive of source and the accusative of merit/demerit both fail to land.

5.15.title: अज्ञानेनावृतं ज्ञानम् — the instrumental of veiling
5.15.body: अज्ञानेन is तृतीया विभक्ति ('by ignorance'), and आवृतम् is the क्त-participle of आ+√वृ ('covered, veiled') qualifying ज्ञानम् (nom.). तेन ('by that') is the instrumental of cause for the finite मुह्यन्ति (लट्, √मुह्, 'are deluded') of जन्तवः — the chain instrumental→covered→deluded is what makes creatures wrongly assign agency.

5.16.title: येषाम् ... नाशितम् — the genitive of the destroyed ignorance
5.16.body: येषाम् is षष्ठी बहुवचन ('of whom'), and नाशितम् is the क्त-participle of णिच्-causative √नश् ('caused to perish') agreeing with neuter तत् अज्ञानम्. ज्ञानेन (तृतीया, 'by knowledge') is the instrument — the relative clause builds a tight causal triangle: knowledge as instrument, ignorance as patient, the wise as locus.

5.16.title: आदित्यवत् — the sun-simile for revealed Brahman
5.16.body: आदित्यवत् is the तद्धित suffix -वत् ('like the sun') functioning adverbially with प्रकाशयति (लट् causative of प्र+√काश्, 'makes-to-shine-forth'). तत्परम् ('that Supreme') is the accusative object — ज्ञानम् is itself the subject doing the revealing, just as the sun does not become what it illumines.

5.17.title: Four तत्पुरुष compounds — the fourfold absorption in That
5.17.body: तद्बुद्धयः, तदात्मानः, तन्निष्ठाः, तत्परायणाः — four nominative-plural बहुव्रीहि compounds, each prefixed with तद् ('That', meaning Brahman): 'whose intellect is That, whose self is That, whose ground-of-standing is That, whose highest-resort is That'. The grammatical fourfold prepares the finite verb.

5.17.title: गच्छन्ति अपुनरावृत्तिम् — accusative of the no-return state
5.17.body: अपुनरावृत्तिम् is the accusative object of गच्छन्ति (लट्, √गम्, 'they go'), itself a नञ्-तत्पुरुष ('non-return'). ज्ञाननिर्धूतकल्मषाः ('those-by-whom-impurity-has-been-shaken-off-by-knowledge') is a long बहुव्रीहि qualifying the subject — the absence-of-return is grammatically reached only after the impurity-clause has done its compound work.

5.18.title: पण्डिताः समदर्शिनः — the verse Chapter 5 is named for
5.18.body: Five सप्तमी-विभक्ति locatives stack: विद्याविनयसम्पन्ने ब्राह्मणे, गवि, हस्तिनि, शुनि, श्वपाके ('in a learned-and-humble Brahmin, in a cow, in an elephant, in a dog, in a dog-cooker'). The predicate समदर्शिनः ('equal-visioned ones') is nominative plural — the wise see the same in five wildly unequal locatives, which is the entire point of the locative pile-up.

5.18.title: विद्याविनयसम्पन्ने — the most loaded locative in the list
5.18.body: विद्याविनयसम्पन्न is a long तत्पुरुष-cum-बहुव्रीहि compound ('endowed-with-learning-and-humility') agreeing with the सप्तमी ब्राह्मणे. Placing the most exalted candidate first and the श्वपाक last is a deliberate rhetorical bracketing — the locatives traverse the entire varṇa-and-species ladder, and समदर्शिनः refuses every rung as a basis for differential darśana.

5.19.title: इहैव जितः सर्गः — saṃsāra defeated in this very life
5.19.body: इह एव ('right here, in this very [life]') is the locative-adverbial, तैः is तृतीया बहुवचन ('by them'), and जितः is the क्त-participle of √जि ('conquered') agreeing with nominative सर्गः ('cycle-of-birth'). The passive construction puts सर्ग in the subject slot — saṃsāra itself is the patient being defeated, here-and-now.

5.19.title: ब्रह्मणि स्थिताः — locative of where the equal-minded stand
5.19.body: ब्रह्मणि is सप्तमी विभक्ति, and स्थिताः is the क्त-participle of √स्था ('standing') agreeing with ते. The verse gives the causal chain: साम्ये स्थितं मनः (locative again, 'mind stationed in equality') → therefore निर्दोषं समं ब्रह्म → therefore ब्रह्मणि स्थिताः. Equality-of-mind is the locative bridge to standing-in-Brahman.

5.20.title: Two विधिलिङ् verbs — the prescriptive grammar of equanimity
5.20.body: न प्रहृष्येत् and न उद्विजेत् are both विधिलिङ् (3rd sg., from प्र+√हृष् 'rejoice' and उद्+√विज् 'be agitated') — the mood prescribes what one ought to do upon प्राप्य प्रियम् / प्राप्य अप्रियम् (absolutives, 'having obtained the pleasant / the unpleasant'). The grammar makes equanimity a normative ought, not a description.

5.20.title: ब्रह्मविद् ब्रह्मणि स्थितः — knower-of-Brahman standing in Brahman
5.20.body: ब्रह्मविद् is a compact उपपद-तत्पुरुष ('Brahman-knower') and ब्रह्मणि स्थितः is the locative + क्त-participle pair from 5.19, now reused. The line stacks three nominative qualifications — स्थिरबुद्धिः, असम्मूढः, ब्रह्मविद् — all converging on the locative final ब्रह्मणि स्थितः: every epithet ends in the same locative resting-place.

5.21.title: बाह्यस्पर्शेषु असक्तात्मा — the locative of what he is not attached to
5.21.body: बाह्यस्पर्शेषु is सप्तमी विभक्ति बहुवचन ('in/to outward contacts'), and असक्तात्मा is a बहुव्रीहि ('he whose self is unattached') governing it. The finite verb विन्दति (लट्, √विद्, 'finds') has the accusative यत्सुखम् ('whatever happiness') and the locative आत्मनि ('in the Self') — outward locative is renounced, inward locative is where the happiness is found.

5.21.title: अक्षयम् अश्नुते — the imperishable enjoyed
5.21.body: ब्रह्मयोगयुक्तात्मा is a long बहुव्रीहि ('he whose self is yoked-by-yoga-with-Brahman'). सुखम् अक्षयम् ('imperishable happiness') is the accusative object of अश्नुते (लट् आत्मनेपद of √अश्, 'enjoys/attains'). The negated क्षय-participle अक्षय ('non-decaying') is what distinguishes this सुख from the आद्यन्तवन्तः भोगाः of the very next verse.

5.22.title: संस्पर्शजाः भोगाः — born-of-contact, doomed by birth
5.22.body: संस्पर्शजाः is a उपपद-तत्पुरुष ('contact-born') agreeing with nominative भोगाः ('enjoyments'). The predicate noun दुःखयोनयः ('having sorrow as their womb') is a बहुव्रीहि — the grammar runs sorrow back upstream to the very origin: contact-born enjoyments have दुःख as their जन्म-source.

5.22.title: आद्यन्तवन्तः — the matvarthīya suffix of finitude
5.22.body: आद्यन्तवन्तः uses the तद्धित suffix -वत् ('possessing') on आदि+अन्त ('beginning + end'), yielding 'possessing-beginning-and-end' — i.e., finite. तेषु is सप्तमी विभक्ति governed by रमते (लट् आत्मनेपद of √रम्, 'delights-in'), and बुधः (nom., 'the awakened one') is its negated subject — the wise locative-delight in no finite thing.

5.23.title: प्राक्शरीरविमोक्षणात् — ablative of the deadline
5.23.body: प्राक् ('before') governs the पञ्चमी विभक्ति शरीरविमोक्षणात् ('from-the-release-of-the-body') — together they form an ablative-of-time-limit: 'before the body's release'. इह एव ('here itself') intensifies the temporal locus. Tolerating the vega of kāma-krodha is given a hard deadline by the ablative case itself.

5.23.title: कामक्रोधोद्भवं वेगम् — accusative of the surge to be endured
5.23.body: कामक्रोधोद्भवम् is a long उपपद-तत्पुरुष ('arising-from-desire-and-anger') agreeing with the accusative वेगम् ('surge, impulse'). सोढुम् is the तुमुन्-infinitive of √सह् ('to endure'), governed by शक्नोति (लट्, √शक्, 'is able'). The yogī's test is grammatically a single infinitive: to-endure the accusative surge before the ablative deadline.

5.24.title: Three अन्तर्-prefixed बहुव्रीहिs — the inward triplet
5.24.body: अन्तःसुखः, अन्तरारामः, अन्तर्ज्योतिः — three nominative बहुव्रीहि compounds ('he whose happiness is within, whose pleasure-grove is within, whose light is within'). The thrice-repeated अन्तर्- prefix shifts the locus of सुख, आराम, and ज्योतिः off the external entirely — every site of value is grammatically interiorized.

5.24.title: ब्रह्मभूतः ... ब्रह्मनिर्वाणम् अधिगच्छति — becoming, then attaining
5.24.body: ब्रह्मभूतः is the क्त-participle of √भू ('having-become-Brahman'), a sequential prerequisite to अधिगच्छति (लट् of अधि+√गम्, 'attains') taking ब्रह्मनिर्वाणम् as its accusative object. The participle and finite verb together encode the order: one first becomes Brahman, then reaches brahma-nirvāṇa — the becoming is structurally prior to the attaining.

5.25.title: क्षीणकल्मषाः, छिन्नद्वैधाः, यतात्मानः — three k̥ta-participle bahuvrīhis
5.25.body: Three nominative-plural बहुव्रीहि compounds qualify ऋषयः: क्षीणकल्मषाः ('whose impurities are exhausted'), छिन्नद्वैधाः ('whose doubt-duality is cut'), यतात्मानः ('whose self is restrained'). Each is built on a क्त-participle (क्षीण, छिन्न, यत) describing a completed inward operation — the grammatical pre-conditions of लभन्ते (लट् आत्मनेपद of √लभ्, 'they obtain').

5.25.title: सर्वभूतहिते रताः — the locative of universal benefit
5.25.body: सर्वभूतहिते is सप्तमी विभक्ति of the तत्पुरुष ('in the welfare-of-all-beings'), and रताः is the क्त-participle of √रम् ('delighted-in') agreeing with ऋषयः. The same रम्-root that 5.22 used negatively (न तेषु रमते) is here used positively — the locative of delight is shifted from the आद्यन्तवन्त भोग to सर्वभूतहित.

5.26.title: Three षष्ठी-plural bahuvrīhis — the genitive parade
5.26.body: कामक्रोधवियुक्तानाम्, यतीनाम् यतचेतसाम्, विदितात्मनाम् — all षष्ठी विभक्ति बहुवचन ('of the desire-and-anger-disjoined, of the restrained-minded ascetics, of the self-known'). The whole verse is a single genitive cluster modifying the locus of ब्रह्मनिर्वाण — grammar makes brahma-nirvāṇa a possession that belongs to those people.

5.26.title: अभितः वर्तते — the adverb of all-around presence
5.26.body: अभितः ('on all sides, all around') is the adverb governing वर्तते (लट् आत्मनेपद of √वृत्, 'exists/is-present'). ब्रह्मनिर्वाणम् is the nominative subject — not something they go to later, but something that exists circling them now. The genitive-plural possession + the all-around-adverb together give an immediate, ambient nirvāṇa.

5.27.title: स्पर्शान् बहिः कृत्वा — accusative-plus-absolutive of expulsion
5.27.body: बाह्यान् स्पर्शान् ('outward contacts') is the accusative-plural object, and कृत्वा (absolutive of √कृ) takes the adverbial बहिः ('outside') as its predicate complement — literally 'having made the outward contacts outside'. The construction is reflexive: the contacts are not destroyed but spatially relocated.

5.27.title: प्राणापानौ समौ कृत्वा — the dual breath equalized
5.27.body: प्राणापानौ is द्वन्द्व dual ('the in-and-out breath'), समौ is the dual predicate adjective ('equal'), and कृत्वा is again the absolutive of √कृ — 'having made the two breaths equal'. नासाभ्यन्तरचारिणौ ('the two moving-within-the-nostril') is a final दुअल बहुव्रीहि locating the equalized breaths precisely between the brows where the eyes were just fixed.

5.28.title: यतेन्द्रियमनोबुद्धिः — one giant bahuvrīhi of full restraint
5.28.body: यतेन्द्रियमनोबुद्धिः packs three controlled faculties — इन्द्रिय, मनस्, बुद्धि — into one nominative-singular बहुव्रीहि headed by the क्त-participle यत ('restrained'). The compound spans the entire psycho-sensory apparatus in a single word, qualifying the subject मुनिः.

5.28.title: विगतेच्छाभयक्रोधः ... मुक्त एव सः — already liberated
5.28.body: विगतेच्छाभयक्रोधः is another long बहुव्रीहि ('he from whom desire, fear, and anger have departed'), and मोक्षपरायणः is a तत्पुरुष ('intent-on-liberation'). The startling predicate मुक्त एव सः ('he is liberated, just so') uses एव to collapse the future tense one might expect — सदा ('always') with मुक्त (क्त-participle) makes the liberation grammatically present, not pending.

5.29.title: Three accusative epithets — knowing Me in three roles
5.29.body: भोक्तारम् यज्ञतपसाम् ('the enjoyer of sacrifices and austerities' — षष्ठी विभक्ति), सर्वलोकमहेश्वरम् ('the great Lord of all worlds'), सुहृदं सर्वभूतानाम् ('the friend of all beings' — षष्ठी again). All three accusatives are direct objects of ज्ञात्वा (absolutive of √ज्ञा, 'having known'), and all coreferent with माम् ('Me').

5.29.title: शान्तिम् ऋच्छति — the chapter's closing finite verb
5.29.body: The whole verse hinges on ज्ञात्वा (absolutive) feeding into ऋच्छति (लट्, √ऋ, 'attains, reaches') with the accusative object शान्तिम्. The ablative-of-liberation that has run through 5.13–5.28 — released from desire, anger, fruit, contact, doubt — finally lands grammatically: the only finite verb is शान्तिम् ऋच्छति, and the sole condition is the prior absolutive ज्ञात्वा माम्.
